steps.title: Interpret as a quadratic in z to the ninth power and set up factorization
steps.content:
The expression $38z^{18} + bz^9 + 28$ is a quadratic in $z^9$. If $qz^9 + r$ is a factor, then: $$38z^{18} + bz^9 + 28 = (qz^9 + r)(az^9 + c)$$
for positive integers $a$ and $c$.

steps.title: Expand and match coefficients to find constraints on b
steps.content:
Expanding the right side: $$(qz^9 + r)(az^9 + c) = qaz^{18} + (qc + ra)z^9 + rc$$ Matching with the original expression:
  • $z^{18}$: $qa = 38$
  • $z^9$: $qc + ra = b$
  • Constant: $rc = 28$
Therefore $b = qc + ra$, where $(q, a)$ and $(r, c)$ are factor pairs of 38 and 28 respectively.

steps.title: Maximize b by testing extreme factor pairings
steps.content:
To maximize $b = qc + ra$, test divisor pairings at the extremes.

When $(q, a) = (1, 38)$ and $(r, c) = (28, 1)$: $$b = 1 \cdot 1 + 28 \cdot 38 = 1 + 1064 = 1065$$ When $(q, a) = (38, 1)$ and $(r, c) = (1, 28)$: $$b = 38 \cdot 28 + 1 \cdot 1 = 1064 + 1 = 1065$$ Other factor pairs (such as $(2, 19)$ paired with divisors of 28) produce smaller sums.

The greatest possible value of $b$ is 1065.
